docs.title: Tileverse
docs.text:
Tileverse provides a toolkit of independent Java libraries for building cloud-native geospatial applications.

These libraries are designed to be composable: pick the ones you need for your specific problem—whether that's reading from cloud storage, handling vector tiles, or calculating tile grids—without pulling in a monolithic framework.

docs.title: Libraries
docs.text: Library Artifact Role Storage tileverse-storage-all Object Storage Abstraction: Full container API (list, range/streaming reads, atomic writes, deletes, server-side copy, presigned URLs) across local files, HTTP, S3 (GP + Express), Azure (Blob + Data Lake Gen2), and GCS (flat + HNS). Includes the byte-range RangeReader API used by PMTiles, COG, and single-file Parquet. PMTiles tileverse-pmtiles Archive Format: Read support for PMTiles v3, leveraging Storage / RangeReader for cloud access. Vector Tiles tileverse-vectortiles Codec: High-performance encoding and decoding of Mapbox Vector Tiles (MVT) to/from JTS Geometries. Tile Matrix Set tileverse-tilematrixset Math & Logic: Implementation of the OGC Tile Matrix Set standard for calculating tile pyramids and grids.

docs.title: Ecosystem
docs.text:
While tileverse-pmtiles naturally uses the other libraries, Storage, Vector Tiles, and Tile Matrix Set are completely standalone.

  • Building a GeoParquet datastore that needs to list and read partitioned files in S3 / Azure / GCS? Use Storage.
  • Reading COGs (Cloud Optimized GeoTIFFs) with byte-range requests? Use Storage (its RangeReader API) directly.
  • Building a tile server from PostGIS? Use Vector Tiles and Tile Matrix Set.
  • Need a standard grid definition? Use Tile Matrix Set.
graph TD
    App[Your App]

    subgraph "I/O"
        ST[Storage<br/>+ RangeReader API]
    end

    subgraph "Formats"
        VT[Vector Tiles]
        PMT[PMTiles]
    end

    subgraph "Spatial"
        TMS[Tile Matrix Set]
    end

    App --> ST
    App --> VT
    App --> PMT
    App --> TMS

    PMT -.-> ST
    PMT -.-> VT
    PMT -.-> TMS

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Tileverse is available on Maven Central. You can use the Bill of Materials (BOM) to manage versions across all modules.

MavenGradle

Add the BOM to your dependencyManagement to ensure version consistency:

<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.tileverse</groupId>
            <artifactId>tileverse-bom</artifactId>
            <version>2.0.0</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

Then add specific modules to your dependencies block:

<dependencies>
    <!-- Efficient data access (File, HTTP, S3, etc.) -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>io.tileverse.storage</groupId>
        <artifactId>tileverse-storage-all</artifactId>
    </dependency>

    <!-- PMTiles support -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>io.tileverse.pmtiles</groupId>
        <artifactId>tileverse-pmtiles</artifactId>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

dependencies {
    implementation(platform("io.tileverse:tileverse-bom:2.0.0"))

    implementation("io.tileverse.storage:tileverse-storage-all")
    implementation("io.tileverse.pmtiles:tileverse-pmtiles")
}

docs.title: Version Compatibility
docs.text: Library Version Java Version Maven Version 2.0.x 17+ 3.9+

docs.title: Reading a PMTiles Archive
docs.text:
The simplest form is PMTilesReader.open(URI), which splits the leaf URI into parent + key, opens the underlying Storage, gets the RangeReader, and bundles them all so closing the reader releases everything.

import io.tileverse.pmtiles.PMTilesReader;
import java.net.URI;
import java.nio.ByteBuffer;
import java.nio.file.Path;
import java.util.Optional;

public class Example {
    public void readTile() throws Exception {
        URI leaf = Path.of("data/planet.pmtiles").toUri();

        try (PMTilesReader reader = PMTilesReader.open(leaf)) {

            // Fetch a specific tile (z=0, x=0, y=0)
            Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(0, 0, 0);
            tile.ifPresent(buffer -> {
                buffer.flip(); // Important: flip the buffer before reading
                System.out.println("Found tile: " + buffer.remaining() + " bytes");
            });
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Cloud Storage Access
docs.text:
To access files on S3, point StorageFactory.open at an s3:// URI for the bucket (or bucket+prefix), then read the leaf URI through the Storage. The upper-level PMTilesReader API remains unchanged.

import io.tileverse.storage.RangeReader;
import io.tileverse.storage.Storage;
import io.tileverse.storage.StorageFactory;
import io.tileverse.storage.cache.CachingRangeReader;
import java.net.URI;
import java.util.Properties;

Properties props = new Properties();
props.setProperty("storage.s3.region", "us-east-1");

URI bucket = URI.create("s3://my-bucket/maps/");
URI leaf = URI.create("s3://my-bucket/maps/planet.pmtiles");

try (Storage storage = StorageFactory.open(bucket, props);
        RangeReader s3Source = storage.openRangeReader(leaf);

        // Wrap with in-memory caching for performance
        RangeReader cachedSource = CachingRangeReader.builder(s3Source)
            .capacity(50_000_000) // 50MB cache
            .build();
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(cachedSource)) {
    // ...
}

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Java 17 or later.
  • Maven 3.8+ or Gradle 7+.

docs.title: Developer Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Tileverse Developer Guide. This section provides everything you need to contribute to the project, build from source, and understand the testing infrastructure.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Building: How to compile and package the project.
  • Testing: Running unit, integration, and performance tests.
  • Contributing: Code style, pull request process, and contribution guidelines.
  • Architecture: High-level system design and module relationships.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
• Modularity: Libraries are designed to work independently.
  • Cloud-Native: First-class support for S3, Azure, GCS, and HTTP.
  • Performance: Heavy focus on zero-copy I/O and efficient memory usage.
  • Standards: Strict adherence to OGC and Mapbox specifications.

docs.title: Building
docs.text: Instructions for building the Tileverse monorepo from source.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Java 21+: For development (the runtime requirement is Java 17+).
  • Maven 3.9+: Build tool and dependency management.
  • Git: Version control.
  • Docker: Required for running integration tests (Testcontainers).

docs.title: Using the Makefile (Recommended)
docs.text:
The project includes a Makefile to simplify common tasks:

# Build everything (compile + test)
make

# Clean build artifacts
make clean

# Compile only
make compile

# Run all tests
make test

# Format code (Spotless + SortPOM)
make format

docs.title: Using Maven Directly
docs.text:
# Build and package
./mvnw clean package

# Skip tests for faster build
./mvnw clean package -DskipTests

docs.title: Module Specific Builds
docs.text:
You can build specific modules to save time:

# Build Range Reader only
./mvnw clean package -pl tileverse-storage

# Build PMTiles only
./mvnw clean package -pl tileverse-pmtiles

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
We enforce strict code quality standards using:

  • Spotless: For Java code formatting (Palantir style).
  • SortPOM: For consistent pom.xml ordering.
  • License Maven Plugin: To ensure file headers are present.
# Check code quality
make lint

# Apply fixes automatically
make format

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Tileverse! We welcome contributions from the community to help build the best Java ecosystem for cloud-native geospatial data.

docs.title: Code Contribution Process
docs.text:
1. Fork & Clone: Fork the repo and clone it locally.
  2. Branch: Create a feature branch (feat/my-feature or fix/my-bug).
  3. Code: Implement your changes.
  4. Test: Add unit tests for new logic and ensure existing tests pass.
  5. Format: Run make format to ensure code style compliance.
  6. PR: Submit a Pull Request to the main branch.

docs.title: Coding Standards
docs.text:
• Java Version: Target Java 17 compatibility, but build with Java 21+.
  • Style: We use the Palantir Java Style.
  • Javadoc: Public APIs must be documented.
  • Null Safety: Use Optional<T> for return types that might be missing. Avoid returning null.

docs.title: Testing Requirements
docs.text:
• Unit Tests: Required for all new logic.
  • Integration Tests: Required for cloud provider interactions (S3, Azure, GCS).
  • Performance Tests: Required if modifying critical I/O paths.

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
We follow the Conventional Commits specification:

  • feat: add GCS support
  • fix: handle EOF correctly in range reader
  • docs: update usage examples
  • chore: bump dependencies

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Releases are automated via GitHub Actions.

  • Snapshots: Published on every merge to main.
  • Releases: Published when a new tag (e.g., v1.0.0) is pushed.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: This guide covers the testing strategy for the Tileverse project.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
We use a tiered testing approach:

  1. Unit Tests: Fast, in-memory tests using JUnit 5 and Mockito.
  2. Integration Tests: Docker-based tests using Testcontainers to verify interactions with real services (S3, Azure, GCS, HTTP).
  3. Benchmarks: JMH microbenchmarks for critical paths.

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
make test-unit
# OR
./mvnw test

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
Requires Docker to be running.

make test-it
# OR
./mvnw verify

docs.title: Specific Modules
docs.text:
# Run tests for PMTiles only
./mvnw test -pl tileverse-pmtiles

# Run S3 integration tests
./mvnw verify -pl tileverse-storage/s3

docs.title: Testcontainers
docs.text:
We use specific images to emulate cloud environments:

  • AWS S3: localstack/localstack
  • Azure Blob: mcr.microsoft.com/azure-storage/azurite
  • GCS: fsouza/fake-gcs-server
  • HTTP: nginx:alpine

docs.title: Performance Benchmarks
docs.text:
Benchmarks are located in the tileverse-storage/benchmarks module.

# Build benchmarks
make build-benchmarks

# Run
make benchmarks

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text: Tileverse is designed as a collection of loosely coupled, composable libraries. While they work seamlessly together, each module acts as a standalone tool for its specific domain (I/O, Tiling, Encodings).

docs.title: Component Relationships
docs.text:
The following diagram illustrates how the libraries relate to user applications and each other. Note that pmtiles is the only module that strictly depends on others (tileverse-storage for I/O and vectortiles for decoding).

graph TD
    subgraph "Application Layer"
        App[User Application]
    end

    subgraph "Independent Modules"
        ST[tileverse-storage<br/>Storage + RangeReader]
        VT[tileverse-vectortiles]
        TMS[tileverse-tilematrixset]
    end

    subgraph "Composed Modules"
        PMT[tileverse-pmtiles]
    end

    App --> ST
    App --> VT
    App --> TMS
    App --> PMT

    PMT --> ST
    PMT --> VT
    PMT --> TMS

docs.title: 1. I/O Independence (tileverse-storage)
docs.text:
We treat data access as a distinct problem from data format.

  • Goal: Read (and write, list, copy, presign) bytes from anywhere (S3, HTTP, Azure, GCS, File) efficiently.
  • Anti-Pattern: Format libraries (like a GeoTIFF reader) implementing their own S3 clients.
  • Solution: Storage provides a unified container API; its RangeReader API exposes the byte-range read surface used by single-file consumers (PMTiles, COG, single-file Parquet).

docs.title: 2. Pure Mathematical Models (tilematrixset)
docs.text:
Spatial reference systems and grid logic are kept separate from data storage.

  • Goal: Calculate tile coordinates and bounding boxes without external dependencies.
  • Benefit: Can be used by a tile server to calculate grids even if the data source isn't Java-based or uses a different I/O library.

docs.title: 3. Format Specificity (vectortiles, pmtiles)
docs.text:
These libraries handle the parsing and encoding logic for specific file specs.

  • Vector Tiles: Pure Protocol Buffers / JTS transcoding. No I/O logic.
  • PMTiles: Orchestrates RangeReader to fetch specific directory bytes, uses VectorTiles to parse the result, and TileMatrixSet to understand the grid.

docs.title: Direct Usage
docs.text:
Applications often use modules directly:

  • ETL Pipelines: Use vectortiles to convert PostGIS geometry to MVT bytes.
  • Tile Servers: Use tilematrixset to calculate which tiles cover a viewport.
  • Data Access: Use tileverse-storage (its RangeReader API) to fetch partial content from Cloud Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs) stored on S3.

docs.title: Composed Usage
docs.text:
The pmtiles library demonstrates the power of composition:

  1. Accepts a RangeReader interface (polymorphic backend).
  2. Uses HilbertCurve (internal) for index lookup.
  3. Returns raw bytes or uses vectortiles to return parsed geometry.

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text: Tileverse aims to minimize transitive dependencies while leveraging established libraries for core functionality.

docs.title: Core Dependencies
docs.text: Library Usage Module JTS Topology Suite Geometry model (Points, Polygons, etc.) vectortiles, pmtiles Caffeine High-performance in-memory caching storage-core Jackson JSON parsing (metadata, configurations) pmtiles Google Protocol Buffers MVT binary encoding/decoding vectortiles SLF4J Logging abstraction All

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text:
These are only required if you use specific features (e.g., cloud storage).

Library Usage Module AWS SDK for Java v2 S3 Range Reader storage-s3 Azure SDK for Java Azure Blob Range Reader storage-azure Google Cloud Java GCS Range Reader storage-gcs Apache Commons Compress Zstd compression support pmtiles

docs.title: Data Flow Architecture
docs.text: Tileverse allows applications to stream tiled data from remote storage directly to the client (or processing engine) with minimal memory overhead.

docs.title: 1. Request Lifecycle
docs.text:
When a PMTilesReader requests a tile (z, x, y):

  1. Spatial Index Lookup: The reader calculates the Hilbert ID for the requested tile.
  2. Directory Fetch: It checks if the relevant directory section is in memory. If not, it issues a byte-range request to the RangeReader.
  3. Offset Calculation: Using the directory data, it finds the exact byte offset and length of the tile data in the archive.
  4. Data Fetch: It issues a second byte-range request to the RangeReader for the actual tile body.
  5. Decoding: The raw bytes are passed to the VectorTileCodec (if MVT) or returned as-is (if Raster).

docs.title: 2. Caching Strategy
docs.text:
To avoid network latency, the CachingRangeReader implements a two-tier cache:

  • Tier 1 (Metadata): PMTiles directories and headers are highly cacheable and small. They are prioritized in the "Meta" cache.
  • Tier 2 (Data): Actual tile data is cached in a separate "Data" cache (optional), useful for hot areas of the map.

docs.title: 3. Extensibility
docs.text:
The system is designed for extension via interfaces:

  • Implement RangeReader to support a new storage backend (e.g., Hadoop HDFS, FTP).
  • Implement TileMatrixSet to support exotic non-Earth coordinate systems.

docs.title: PMTiles
docs.text:
This module provides a pure Java implementation of the PMTiles v3 specification, a single-file archive format for cloud-optimized tiled data.

This library is focused on efficient, cloud-optimized reading of PMTiles archives. For creating archives, use one of the existing tools such as pmtiles or Tippecanoe.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Cloud-Optimized Reads: Range-based access tuned for archives served from object storage.
  • Storage Agnostic: Works seamlessly with any RangeReader backend from tileverse-storage (S3, HTTP, Local File, Azure Blob, GCS).
  • Spatial Indexing: Implements Hilbert curve indexing for O(log N) tile lookups.
  • Type Support: Handles both vector (MVT) and raster (PNG, JPG, WebP) tiles.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.tileverse.pmtiles</groupId>
    <artifactId>tileverse-pmtiles</artifactId>
</dependency>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
The PMTilesReader accepts either a RangeReader or a Supplier<SeekableByteChannel>. The RangeReader can provide this via its asByteChannel() method.

import io.tileverse.pmtiles.PMTilesHeader;
import io.tileverse.pmtiles.PMTilesReader;
import java.nio.file.Path;

try (PMTilesReader reader = PMTilesReader.open(Path.of("data/map.pmtiles").toUri())) {
    // Get metadata
    PMTilesHeader header = reader.getHeader();
    System.out.println("Min Zoom: " + header.minZoom());

    // Fetch tile (z, x, y)
    reader.getTile(0, 0, 0).ifPresent(buffer -> {
        buffer.flip(); // Important: flip the buffer before reading
        // Process tile bytes...
    });
}

docs.title: PMTiles User Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Tileverse PMTiles User Guide. This guide will help you understand and use the PMTiles library effectively.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
This guide covers:

  • Quick Start: Get up and running quickly
  • Reading PMTiles: Read tiles from PMTiles archives
  • Cloud Storage: Work with PMTiles in S3, Azure, and GCS

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Add the PMTiles dependency to your project:

MavenGradle 
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.tileverse.pmtiles</groupId>
    <artifactId>tileverse-pmtiles</artifactId>
    <version>2.0.0</version>
</dependency>

<!-- Also add a Range Reader provider -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.tileverse.storage</groupId>
    <artifactId>tileverse-storage-all</artifactId>
    <version>2.0.0</version>
</dependency>

dependencies {
    implementation 'io.tileverse.pmtiles:tileverse-pmtiles:2.0.0'
    implementation 'io.tileverse.storage:tileverse-storage-all:2.0.0'
}

docs.title: PMTiles Structure
docs.text:
A PMTiles archive contains:

  1. Header: Metadata about the tileset (bounds, zoom levels, tile format)
  2. Directory: Spatial index using Hilbert curves
  3. Tiles: Compressed tile data

docs.title: Tile Addressing
docs.text:
Tiles are addressed using the standard XYZ scheme:

  • z: Zoom level (0 = world view, higher = more detailed)
  • x: Column number (west to east)
  • y: Row number (north to south in TMS, south to north in XYZ)

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
PMTiles can be read from any source supported by Range Reader:

  • Local files
  • HTTP/HTTPS servers
  • Amazon S3
  • Azure Blob Storage
  • Google Cloud Storage

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start: Begin with a simple example
  • Reading PMTiles: Learn about reading tiles
  • Cloud Storage: Access PMTiles from the cloud

docs.title: Cloud Storage Integration
docs.text: Learn how to efficiently access PMTiles from cloud storage providers.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: PMTiles is designed to work efficiently with cloud object storage. By using HTTP range requests, you can serve tiles directly from S3, Azure Blob Storage, or Google Cloud Storage without a specialized tile server.

docs.title: Basic S3 Access
docs.text:
If the bucket allows the SDK's default credential chain (IAM role, ~/.aws/credentials, env vars), PMTilesReader.open(URI) is a one-liner:

import io.tileverse.pmtiles.PMTilesReader;
import java.net.URI;
import java.util.Optional;

try (PMTilesReader reader = PMTilesReader.open(URI.create("s3://my-bucket/world.pmtiles"))) {
    Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(10, 885, 412);
}

When you need explicit configuration (region pinning, credentials, endpoint override), open the parent {@link Storage} with Properties and ask it for the reader:

import io.tileverse.storage.RangeReader;
import io.tileverse.storage.Storage;
import io.tileverse.storage.StorageFactory;
import java.util.Properties;

Properties props = new Properties();
props.setProperty("storage.s3.region", "us-west-2");

URI bucket = URI.create("s3://my-bucket/");
URI leaf = URI.create("s3://my-bucket/world.pmtiles");
try (Storage storage = StorageFactory.open(bucket, props);
        RangeReader s3Reader = storage.openRangeReader(leaf);
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(s3Reader)) {
    Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(10, 885, 412);
}

docs.title: With Caching
docs.text:
import io.tileverse.storage.cache.CachingRangeReader;

try (Storage storage = StorageFactory.open(bucket, props);
        RangeReader baseReader = storage.openRangeReader(leaf);
        RangeReader cachedReader = CachingRangeReader.builder(baseReader)
            .maximumSize(1000)
            .withBlockAlignment()
            .build();
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(cachedReader)) {
    // Cached reads
    Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(10, 885, 412);
}

docs.title: Azure Blob Storage
docs.text:
Properties azureProps = new Properties();
azureProps.setProperty("storage.azure.connection-string", connectionString);

URI container = URI.create("https://account.blob.core.windows.net/tiles/");
URI leaf = URI.create("https://account.blob.core.windows.net/tiles/world.pmtiles");
try (Storage storage = StorageFactory.open(container, azureProps);
        RangeReader azureReader = storage.openRangeReader(leaf);
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(azureReader)) {
    Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(10, 885, 412);
}

docs.title: Google Cloud Storage
docs.text:
try (PMTilesReader reader = PMTilesReader.open(URI.create("gs://my-bucket/world.pmtiles"))) {
    Optional<ByteBuffer> tile = reader.getTile(10, 885, 412);
}

docs.title: Pre-built SDK clients (escape hatch)
docs.text:
For Spring-managed SDK clients, custom retry policies, or test fakes that the Properties-driven StorageFactory route can't express, each backend provider exposes a public static factory XxxStorageProvider.open(URI, sdkClient) that returns a Storage. The returned Storage borrows the supplied client (close is a no-op), so the caller retains lifetime control:

@Bean Storage tiles(S3Client springS3) {
    return S3StorageProvider.open(URI.create("s3://my-bucket/tiles/"), springS3);
}

// elsewhere:
try (RangeReader r = storage.openRangeReader("00/00.pmtiles");
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(r)) { ... }

docs.title: Multi-Level Caching
docs.text:
Combine memory and disk caching for optimal performance:

import io.tileverse.storage.cache.DiskCachingRangeReader;

try (Storage storage = StorageFactory.open(parent, props);
        RangeReader baseReader = storage.openRangeReader(leaf);
        RangeReader diskCached = DiskCachingRangeReader.builder(baseReader)
            .cacheDirectory(Path.of("/tmp/tile-cache"))
            .maximumCacheSize(10_000_000_000L)  // 10 GB
            .build();
        RangeReader memoryCached = CachingRangeReader.builder(diskCached)
            .maximumSize(1000)
            .build();
        PMTilesReader reader = new PMTilesReader(memoryCached)) {
    // Optimized access
}

docs.title: Block Alignment
docs.text:
Use block-aligned reads to minimize cloud storage requests:

import io.tileverse.storage.block.BlockAlignedRangeReader;

RangeReader alignedReader = BlockAlignedRangeReader.builder(baseReader)
    .blockSize(65536)  // 64 KB blocks
    .build();

docs.title: Cost Optimization
docs.text:
1. Enable caching to reduce request counts
  2. Use CDN in front of object storage
  3. Choose appropriate storage class (Standard vs. Infrequent Access)
  4. Monitor request patterns and adjust caching strategy

docs.text:
